# Liveblocks Collaborative Form (Storage-backed Inputs)

## Background

You are extending a Next.js (App Router) starter that wires up a Liveblocks `RoomProvider` for a single shared collaborative form. The Storage root is intended to be a `LiveObject` with three text fields — `name`, `email`, `message` — plus a `lastUpdate` ISO timestamp. Every keystroke from any connected client must propagate to all other clients through Liveblocks Storage, and the page must show a small badge with the latest ISO timestamp of the most recent write.

The starter scaffolding already pre-installs Node 20, Next.js 14, and the Liveblocks v2 React + Node SDKs. The Liveblocks auth endpoint and `liveblocks.config.ts` types are in place. **The page itself is intentionally not yet wired to Storage** — it currently only renders the static inputs and you must complete the Storage integration.

The app MUST be wired to the real Liveblocks cloud (no mocks, no fake WebSocket, no in-memory stub).

## Requirements

* The collaborative form lives at the route `/` (the App Router root page). All connected clients must join the same Liveblocks room.
* Storage layout (must match exactly): `Storage.form: LiveObject<{ name: string; email: string; message: string; lastUpdate: string }>`.
* The page MUST be a Client Component (or wrap one) since Liveblocks hooks require the client runtime.
* The `RoomProvider` is declared with `initialStorage={{ form: new LiveObject({ name: "", email: "", message: "", lastUpdate: "" }) }}` so a first-visit room starts with empty fields.
* All three inputs must be **two-way bound** to Storage: their visible value is read from `useStorage((root) => root.form.<field>)`, and any user typing into the inputs must call a `useMutation` that writes the new value back to the same `LiveObject` field. Every write must also update `form.lastUpdate` to a fresh `new Date().toISOString()` value.
* The page must render a `<span id="form-last-update">` containing the current `lastUpdate` ISO string from Storage.
* All Liveblocks access must go through the official `@liveblocks/react` provider configured with a server-side authentication endpoint at `/api/liveblocks-auth` that uses `LIVEBLOCKS_SECRET_KEY` and `@liveblocks/node`. `NEXT_PUBLIC_LIVEBLOCKS_PUBLIC_KEY` may also be referenced.
* Use Liveblocks **v2** SDKs (`@liveblocks/client` and `@liveblocks/react` major version 2). Use `LiveObject` for Storage, not bare JS objects.
* The room id MUST be derived from `NEXT_PUBLIC_ZEALT_RUN_ID` at build/runtime. Read the run id from the `ZEALT_RUN_ID` environment variable, re-export it as `NEXT_PUBLIC_ZEALT_RUN_ID=$ZEALT_RUN_ID` BEFORE running `npm run build` and `npm run start`, and use it inside the client as the room id: `form-storage-${NEXT_PUBLIC_ZEALT_RUN_ID}`. (The verifier ALWAYS starts the server itself, so make sure you do **NOT** leave a development server running.)
* **You MUST kill any dev server** (`npm run dev`, `next dev`, background `npm run start`) that you started during implementation before signaling completion. The verifier launches its own production server on port `3000`; a leftover process will conflict with it.

## Implementation Hints

* Use `useStorage((root) => root.form.name)` (and equivalents) to read each field; use `useMutation` to write back into the `LiveObject` via `storage.get("form").set("name", value)`.
* A single `useMutation` that takes `(field, value)` is enough to update any of the three text fields plus `lastUpdate`.
* For Next.js App Router, wrap the page with a `"use client"` boundary; use `@liveblocks/react/suspense` and `ClientSideSuspense` while Storage loads.
* The Liveblocks v2 React SDK uses global types via `declare global { interface Liveblocks { Storage: ... } }`, which is already declared for you in `liveblocks.config.ts`.
* In the auth route, use `new Liveblocks({ secret }).prepareSession(...).allow(room, session.FULL_ACCESS)` and return the body from `session.authorize()` — this is the v2 server SDK API.
* Do **not** add `NEXT_PUBLIC_ZEALT_RUN_ID` or `ZEALT_RUN_ID` to `liveblocks.config.ts`. The room id wiring belongs in the page / room provider.

## Acceptance Criteria

* Project path: `/home/user/project`
* Start command: `npm run start` (after `npm run build`). The verifier sets `NEXT_PUBLIC_ZEALT_RUN_ID=$ZEALT_RUN_ID` before running both `build` and `start`.
* Port: `3000`
* Routes:
  * `GET /` returns the collaborative form HTML.
  * `POST /api/liveblocks-auth` returns HTTP 200 with a non-empty JSON body containing a `token` field minted by `@liveblocks/node` against the real Liveblocks API. Two consecutive calls must return different tokens.
* DOM contract (these are the only stable selectors used by verification):
  * `<input id="form-name">` — collaborative name text input.
  * `<input id="form-email">` — collaborative email text input.
  * `<textarea id="form-message">` — collaborative message textarea.
  * `<span id="form-last-update">` — shows the current `lastUpdate` ISO string from Storage. Empty string is allowed before the first edit.
* Behavior (verified across two browser contexts sharing the same room):
  * Any keystroke by context A in any field is reflected in the same field on context B within a few seconds.
  * The reverse holds: edits by B are seen by A.
  * After at least one edit, `#form-last-update` contains a value that parses as ISO-8601 (e.g. matches `YYYY-MM-DDTHH:MM:SS(.sss)?Z` or equivalent ISO format).
  * When context A reloads the page after both clients disconnect, the `name`/`email`/`message` it sees on first paint match the last persisted values — i.e. Storage is durable.
* No mocks: `@liveblocks/client`, `@liveblocks/react`, and `@liveblocks/node` must remain real dependencies. There must be no `jest.mock(...)`/`vi.mock(...)` of any `@liveblocks/*` module anywhere under the project source.
* Real Liveblocks cloud only — no fake WebSocket, no shadowed packages under `node_modules/@liveblocks/*`.
Let me start by exploring the project structure to understand what's already in place.

todos.content: Create RoomProvider component with Liveblocks configuration
Update page.tsx to use Storage-backed inputs with useStorage and useMutation
Verify liveblocks-auth route is correct
Run npm run build to verify the app compiles

part.text:
Now I'll create the Liveblocks room provider file and update the page. I need to:

1. Create a `LiveblocksProvider` component that wires up the `createClient` with the auth endpoint
2. Create a `RoomProvider` wrapper component
3. Update the page to use `useStorage` and `useMutation`
Now I need to look at how the Liveblocks v2 React SDK exports hooks to create a properly typed set of hooks:

The Liveblocks v2 React SDK uses a global types approach with `declare global { interface Liveblocks { ... } }`. The hooks (`useStorage`, `useMutation`, `RoomProvider`) are exported directly from `@liveblocks/react` and `@liveblocks/react/suspense`. I'll use `createRoomContext` to get typed hooks, or simply use the global hooks directly.

Let me check what `createRoomContext` returns vs using global hooks:

The v2 SDK uses global types - the hooks are typed via `declare global { interface Liveblocks { ... } }` which is already set in `liveblocks.config.ts`. So I can import the hooks directly from `@liveblocks/react` and `@liveblocks/react/suspense`.

Now I'll write the updated page that uses Liveblocks Storage:

todos.content: Create RoomProvider component with Liveblocks configuration
Update page.tsx to use Storage-backed inputs with useStorage and useMutation
Verify liveblocks-auth route is correct
Run npm run build to verify the app compiles

part.text: Now update the main page to use Liveblocks Storage hooks:

todos.content: Create RoomProvider component with Liveblocks configuration
Update page.tsx to use Storage-backed inputs with useStorage and useMutation
Verify liveblocks-auth route is correct
Run npm run build to verify the app compiles

part.text: The auth route looks correct. Let me verify it handles the room ID correctly — the page will send the room ID via the auth endpoint. Let me check how the Liveblocks v2 React SDK calls the auth endpoint:

The auth endpoint is configured in the client (`createClient`). The SDK will call `POST /api/liveblocks-auth` with the room ID in the request body. The current auth route correctly reads `body.room` and grants access. This looks correct.

Now let me also check that I need to ensure the `liveblocks-client.ts` file exports properly — actually, looking at my implementation again, I realize the `createClient` call in `liveblocks-client.ts` is not being used. In Liveblocks v2, the `RoomProvider` from `@liveblocks/react/suspense` is a global provider that uses global types, and the client configuration needs to be passed to `LiveblocksProvider` or via `createClient`.

Let me re-check how Liveblocks v2 React hooks work:

output.content:
*/ useMyPresence(): [ P, (patch: Partial

, options?: { addToHistory: boolean; }) => void ]; /** * useUpdateMyPresence is similar to useMyPresence but it only returns the function to update the current user presence. * If you don't use the current user presence in your component, but you need to update it (e.g. live cursor), it's better to use useUpdateMyPresence to avoid unnecessary renders. * * @example * const updateMyPresence = useUpdateMyPresence(); * updateMyPresence({ x: 0 }); * updateMyPresence({ y: 0 }); * * // At the next render, the presence of the current user will be equal to "{ x: 0, y: 0 }" */ useUpdateMyPresence(): (patch: Partial

, options?: { addToHistory: boolean; }) => void; /** * Create a callback function that lets you mutate Liveblocks state. * * The first argument that gets passed into your callback will be * a "mutation context", which exposes the following: * * - `storage` - The mutable Storage root. * You can mutate any Live structures with this, for example: * `storage.get('layers').get('layer1').set('fill', 'red')` * * - `setMyPresence` - Call this with a new (partial) Presence value. * * - `self` - A read-only version of the latest self, if you need it to * compute the next state. * * - `others` - A read-only version of the latest others list, if you * need it to compute the next state. * * useMutation is like React's useCallback, except that the first argument * that gets passed into your callback will be a "mutation context". * * If you want get access to the immutable root somewhere in your mutation, * you can use `storage.ToImmutable()`. * * @example * const fillLayers = useMutation( * ({ storage }, color: Color) => { * ... * }, * [], * ); * * fillLayers('red'); * * const deleteLayers = useMutation( * ({ storage }) => { * ... * }, * [], * ); * * deleteLayers(); */ useMutation, ...args: any[]) => any>(callback: F, deps: readonly unknown[]): OmitFirstArg; /** * Returns an array with information about all the users currently connected * in the room (except yourself). * * @example * const others = useOthers(); * * // Example to map all cursors in JSX * return ( * <> * {others.map((user) => { * if (user.presence.cursor == null) { * return null; * } * return * })} * * ) */ useOthers(): readonly User[]; /** * Extract arbitrary data based on all the users currently connected in the * room (except yourself). * * The selector function will get re-evaluated any time a user enters or * leaves the room, as well as whenever their presence data changes. * * The component that uses this hook will automatically re-render if your * selector function returns a different value from its previous run. * * By default `useOthers()` uses strict `===` to check for equality. Take * extra care when returning a computed object or list, for example when you * return the result of a .map() or .filter() call from the selector. In * those cases, you'll probably want to use a `shallow` comparison check. * * @example * const avatars = useOthers(users => users.map(u => u.info.avatar), shallow); * const cursors = useOthers(users => users.map(u => u.presence.cursor), shallow); * const someoneIsTyping = useOthers(users => users.some(u => u.presence.isTyping)); * */ useOthers(selector: (others: readonly User[]) => T, isEqual?: (prev: T, curr: T) => boolean): T; /** * Returns an array of connection IDs. This matches the values you'll get by * using the `useOthers()` hook. * * Roughly equivalent to: * useOthers((others) => others.map(other => other.connectionId), shallow) * * This is useful in particular to implement efficiently rendering components * for each user in the room, e.g. cursors. * * @example * const ids = useOthersConnectionIds(); * // [2, 4, 7] */ useOthersConnectionIds(): readonly number[]; /** * Related to useOthers(), but optimized for selecting only "subsets" of * others. This is useful for performance reasons in particular, because * selecting only a subset of users also means limiting the number of * re-renders that will be triggered. * * @example * const avatars = useOthersMapped(user => user.info.avatar); * // ^^^^^^^ * // { connectionId: number; data: string }[] * * The selector function you pass to useOthersMapped() is called an "item * selector", and operates on a single user at a time. If you provide an * (optional) "item comparison" function, it will be used to compare each * item pairwise. * * For example, to select multiple properties: * * @example * const avatarsAndCursors = useOthersMapped( * user => [u.info.avatar, u.presence.cursor], * shallow, // 👈 * ); */ useOthersMapped(itemSelector: (other: User) => T, itemIsEqual?: (prev: T, curr: T) => boolean): ReadonlyArray; /** * Given a connection ID (as obtained by using `useOthersConnectionIds`), you * can call this selector deep down in your component stack to only have the * component re-render if properties for this particular user change.

part.text: Now let me look at the `RoomProvider` and `LiveblocksProvider` usage with global types. The key question is: does the global `RoomProvider` from `@liveblocks/react/suspense` use `createClient` automatically, or does it need a `LiveblocksProvider` wrapping it?

matches.context: * Raw access to the React context where the LiveblocksProvider stores the
* Unlike `RoomProvider`, `LiveblocksProvider` doesn’t call Liveblocks servers when mounted,
declare function LiveblocksProvider<U extends BaseUserMeta = DU>(props: PropsWithChildren<ClientOptions<U>>): react_jsx_runtime.JSX.Element;
* Creates a LiveblocksProvider and a set of typed hooks. Note that any
* LiveblocksProvider created in this way takes no props, because it uses
* LiveblocksProvider, as it already has access to the client.

part.text:
I can see that the `LiveblocksProvider` takes `ClientOptions` as props. In Liveblocks v2, you need to wrap with `LiveblocksProvider` and pass the client options (like `authEndpoint`) to it, or use `createClient` separately and pass it.

Let me look at the `LiveblocksProvider` signature more carefully:

The `LiveblocksProvider` takes `ClientOptions` directly (same as `createClient` options), and `RoomProvider` is the room-level provider. The global hooks from `@liveblocks/react/suspense` should work with `LiveblocksProvider` wrapping `RoomProvider`.

I need to update my `room.tsx` to include `LiveblocksProvider` wrapping `RoomProvider`:
Now let me run the build to check for compilation errors:

Run npm run build to verify the app compiles

Verify liveblocks-auth route is correct
Run npm run build to verify the app compiles

input.result:
The Liveblocks Storage integration is complete. Here's what was implemented:

## Files Created/Modified

### `app/room.tsx` (new)
- Client component with `"use client"` directive
- Wraps children in `LiveblocksProvider` (configured with `authEndpoint: "/api/liveblocks-auth"`) and `RoomProvider`
- Room ID derived from `process.env.NEXT_PUBLIC_ZEALT_RUN_ID` as `form-storage-${NEXT_PUBLIC_ZEALT_RUN_ID}`
- `initialStorage` uses `new LiveObject({ name: "", email: "", message: "", lastUpdate: "" })` under the `form` key

### `app/page.tsx` (rewritten)
- `CollaborativeForm` component uses:
  - `useStorage((root) => root.form.<field>)` to read each of `name`, `email`, `message`, `lastUpdate` from Liveblocks Storage
  - A single `useMutation` (`updateField`) that calls `storage.get("form").set(field, value)` and also updates `lastUpdate` with `new Date().toISOString()` on every keystroke
- All four required DOM elements are present: `#form-name`, `#form-email`, `#form-message`, `#form-last-update`
- Wrapped in `<Room>` + `<ClientSideSuspense>` so Storage loads before rendering inputs

### `app/api/liveblocks-auth/route.ts` (unchanged — already correct)
- Uses `@liveblocks/node` `Liveblocks` class with `LIVEBLOCKS_SECRET_KEY`
- `prepareSession().allow(room, FULL_ACCESS).authorize()` returns a fresh token per call

## Build
The production build (`npm run build`) completes successfully with `✓ Compiled successfully`. No dev server is left running — port 3000 is free for the verifier to start its own production server via `npm run start`.
